results.content:
Meal Vouchers at Hong Kong Disneyland chicken

On the Hong Kong Disneyland Website, the 3-in-1 park meal voucher, or the Lunch and Dinner plus snack voucher, costs HK$315. On Klook, that same voucher costs, as of this writing, HK$293. The Lunch OR Dinner plus snack voucher on Klook costs HK$182, while the child voucher costs HK$106 [...] The former means that you can only purchase either a lunch OR a dinner with the coupon, not both, which can be useful for those who only plan to stay in the park for half the day. The latter means you get both lunch AND dinner with the coupon, which is useful for those who plan to spend the entire day at the park. 

Meal Vouchers at Hong Kong Disneyland pizza

Meal Vouchers at Hong Kong Disneyland pizza

Lunch is defined as a meal from park open until 4pm while dinner is defined as a meal from 4:30pm to closing. The snack is defined as either a frozen ice cream bar or a popcorn from any outdoor vending cart. Additionally, the voucher also includes HK$5 off of any soft serve in the park. [...] You can use the lunch and dinner vouchers in the following quick service restaurants: Starliner Diner and Comet Cafe in Tomorrowland, Clopin’s Festival of Foods and Royal Banquet Hall in Fantasyland, Wayfinder’s Table in Adventureland, and Golden Crocus Inn in World of Frozen. You can claim the popcorn or ice cream at any outdoor vending cart. The HK$5 off on soft serve can be used at Midtown Delights on Main Street, NRG POD in Tomorrowland, Jessie’s Snack Roundup in Toy Story land, Northern Delights in World of Frozen, and Lucky Nugget Saloon in Grizzly Gulch.

![The Disney Specialist Logo](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/09/ds-mobile-logo.png.webp)

## Meal Vouchers at Hong Kong Disneyland Guide: Are They Worth It?

![Meal Vouchers at Hong Kong Disneyland cover](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/HKDL-Trip-Report-2023-Part-One-Introduction-73.png "HKDL Trip Report 2023 Part One Introduction (73)")

If you’re planning a Hong Kong Disneyland (HKDL) trip, you’ve probably come across these meal vouchers on the Hong Kong Disneyland website or on third party websites. At face value, they seem like a good deal. Bundling your lunch, dinner, and a snack at the park seems like it’ll save you a lot of money. But are the meal vouchers at Hong Kong Disneyland really worth it? And if they are, how do you go about using them? Well this post will hopefully answer those questions and more!

![Meal Vouchers at Hong Kong Disneyland me](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/photo_2025-12-17-17.16.35-400x533.jpeg)

![Meal Vouchers at Hong Kong Disneyland me](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/photo_2025-12-17-17.16.35-400x533.jpeg)

I’ve personally used these meal vouchers on recent trips to Hong Kong Disneyland, so I have first hand experience and opinions about this dining deal. In this post I will go into detail on the kind of value these vouchers create, whether they are worth purchasing, and how to use them when you get to the park. 

Let’s begin!

## **What is a Meal Voucher at Hong Kong Disneyland and What’s Included?**

![](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/photo_2025-12-17-17.16.33-600x450.jpeg)

![](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/photo_2025-12-17-17.16.33-600x450.jpeg)

A meal voucher at Hong Kong Disneyland is a coupon that you can use to redeem a lunch meal and/or a dinner meal from a participating quick service restaurant, as well as a snack from any of the many snack carts at the park. They come in two variations: as a Lunch or Dinner Meal + Snack combo and a Lunch AND Dinner meal + snack combo. 

The former means that you can only purchase either a lunch OR a dinner with the coupon, not both, which can be useful for those who only plan to stay in the park for half the day. The latter means you get both lunch AND dinner with the coupon, which is useful for those who plan to spend the entire day at the park. 

![Meal Vouchers at Hong Kong Disneyland pizza](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/photo_2025-12-17-17.16.38-1-600x450.jpeg)

![Meal Vouchers at Hong Kong Disneyland pizza](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/photo_2025-12-17-17.16.38-1-600x450.jpeg)

Lunch is defined as a meal from park open until 4pm while dinner is defined as a meal from 4:30pm to closing. The snack is defined as either a frozen ice cream bar or a popcorn from any outdoor vending cart. Additionally, the voucher also includes HK$5 off of any soft serve in the park. 

Once you purchase the meal vouchers, the vouchers should indicate until when you can use them. Upon checking, the vouchers I bought for my last trip had a validity of three months. There is also a child’s meal voucher that you can get for a lower price, however it only comes with one child’s meal. It does not come with a snack or HK$5 off on soft serve. 

## **Where You Can Buy a Meal Voucher**

![](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/photo_2025-12-17-17.16.38-600x450.jpeg)

![](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/photo_2025-12-17-17.16.38-600x450.jpeg)

You can buy a meal voucher from the Hong Kong Disneyland website and from third party websites like Klook. I would highly recommend buying from Klook as they are usually a little cheaper than buying from the HKDL website. I have an affiliate link you can use to buy these vouchers. Additionally, the 1 meal Lunch or Dinner voucher, as well as the child’s meal voucher, can only be booked on Klook and cannot be found on the HKDL website. So if you want to buy those vouchers, I would highly recommend checking out my link. 

## **How Much the Meal Voucher Costs and Where You Can Use It**

![Meal Vouchers at Hong Kong Disneyland chicken](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/photo_2025-12-17-17.16.40-600x450.jpeg)

![Meal Vouchers at Hong Kong Disneyland chicken](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/photo_2025-12-17-17.16.40-600x450.jpeg)

On the Hong Kong Disneyland Website, the 3-in-1 park meal voucher, or the Lunch and Dinner plus snack voucher, costs HK$315. On Klook, that same voucher costs, as of this writing, HK$293. The Lunch OR Dinner plus snack voucher on Klook costs HK$182, while the child voucher costs HK$106

You can use the lunch and dinner vouchers in the following quick service restaurants: Starliner Diner and Comet Cafe in Tomorrowland, Clopin’s Festival of Foods and Royal Banquet Hall in Fantasyland, Wayfinder’s Table in Adventureland, and Golden Crocus Inn in World of Frozen. You can claim the popcorn or ice cream at any outdoor vending cart. The HK$5 off on soft serve can be used at Midtown Delights on Main Street, NRG POD in Tomorrowland, Jessie’s Snack Roundup in Toy Story land, Northern Delights in World of Frozen, and Lucky Nugget Saloon in Grizzly Gulch.

## **How the Meal Vouchers Work and How to Make the Most Out of Them**

![](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/photo_2025-12-17-17.16.45-600x450.jpeg)

![](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/photo_2025-12-17-17.16.45-600x450.jpeg)

Using these vouchers can be a bit tricky. Each lunch and dinner voucher is worth HK$160. That means that to maximize the value of the voucher, you must purchase a meal worth HK$160 or more at the participating restaurants. Using it on anything less than HK$160 will mean the value of the voucher will degrade and you won’t be able to maximize the HK$50 in savings that the voucher gets you. 

Do note that, if you order something above HK$160, you will have to pay the difference between the voucher and the cost of the meal. So if I order something at a restaurant worth HK$170, I will need to pay HK$10 upon checkout. You’ll still retain the value of the voucher if you do this, however, so I’d recommend buying up instead of buying down. 

![Meal Vouchers at Hong Kong Disneyland pasta](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/photo_2025-12-17-17.16.46-600x450.jpeg)

![Meal Vouchers at Hong Kong Disneyland pasta](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/photo_2025-12-17-17.16.46-600x450.jpeg)

Do note that these vouchers are combo vouchers, meaning they also come with a drink. Based on my experience this can either be a soda fountain drink or bottled water. You could buy one of the specialty juices that the resort sells, but you will need to pay extra for this.

Any of the regular ice creams, such as the Mickey Ice Cream Bar, the Stitch paw bar, and the LinaBell bar, are included in the voucher. Do note that you can only claim one snack a day, so if you want more you’re going to have to pay for the additional ones yourself.

To use the voucher, simply present the coupon to the cast member manning the cashier. They’re going to scan your coupon with a machine and confirm the price of the meal you ordered. If you bought a meal for over the HK$160 amount, they will ask you to pay the price difference.  

## **Should You Buy the Meal Voucher at Hong Kong Disneyland?**

![](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/photo_2025-12-17-17.16.43-600x450.jpeg)

![](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/photo_2025-12-17-17.16.43-600x450.jpeg)

The answer to this question will depend on your budget and your willingness to be flexible. If you’re willing to stick to the meals that are priced at HK$160, then buying the meal vouchers could be well worth it. However, this severely limits the number of meals you can order. Not every dish is priced at HK$160, and most of them are under that, meaning you’ll lose some of the value of the voucher. 

If you’re ok with being limited by the price of the voucher, and don’t mind skipping or not being able to eat some of the meals in the park, then by all means use the voucher. But if you’re the kind of park-goer who likes to explore the different culinary options at the park, then maybe this voucher isn’t for you. 

This voucher is also not for you if you plan on eating more meals at the park. If you wanted a full meal for a snack, for example, then you wouldn’t be able to use the voucher to pay for that meal. However, if you only plan on eating lunch and dinner and using your snack credit to tide you over between the two meals, then this voucher could be useful to you. 

![Meal Vouchers at Hong Kong Disneyland burger](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/photo_2025-12-17-17.16.42-600x450.jpeg)

![Meal Vouchers at Hong Kong Disneyland burger](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/photo_2025-12-17-17.16.42-600x450.jpeg)

If you’re on a tight budget and need to save any way you can, then buying this meal voucher may be worth it for you. HK$50 in savings a day is no small number, and if you’re in a large group that number can really add up. So if you need to save money, I would recommend getting this voucher. 

There’s no one size fits all answer to this question. The answer will depend on your needs and the needs of your group. That being said, I believe in the thought that a Disney vacation should not be expensive. You should not have to pay an arm and a leg to visit the parks. Disney should be accessible to as many people as possible, and if this meal voucher is one way to make the experience more affordable for families, then I’m all for it. 

## **Meal Vouchers at Hong Kong Disneyland Guide Conclusion**

![Meal Vouchers at Hong Kong Disneyland ginseng](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/photo_2025-12-17-17.16.37-600x450.jpeg)

![Meal Vouchers at Hong Kong Disneyland ginseng](https://thedisneyspecialist.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/photo_2025-12-17-17.16.37-600x450.jpeg)

And that’s it! I hope I was able to answer all your questions about meal vouchers at Hong Kong Disneyland. If you have any more questions, feel free to reach out on my socials!

## Other visitors' reviews of Hong Kong Disneyland

Show More Reviews

![紫嫣若琳](https://ak-d.tripcdn.com/images/fd/headphoto/g2/M00/06/AA/Cghzf1TKJ8CAc45-AADN2Wx_DcA238_C_36_36_R5_Q40.webp?proc=source/trip)紫嫣若琳

5.0/5

Outstanding

Hong Kong Disneyland is a perfect destination for families with children. The park is compact, with rest areas and restrooms conveniently located throughout, making it easy to enjoy without much physical exertion. There are many child-friendly attractions, such as Dumbo the Flying Elephant, the carousel, and It's a Small World, which kids love. The Frozen area is a particular favorite among children. There are plenty of shows, plenty of character interaction, and a warm and therapeutic atmosphere. Transportation is convenient, and booking tickets through Ctrip is fast and efficient, ensuring smooth entry. Kids have a great time, and adults can relax – making it a must-visit destination for family trips in Hong Kong.

## Hong Kong Disneyland Highlights: Must-See Features and Attractions

Some information may have been translated by Google Translate

**WORLD OF FROZEN IS NOW OPEN AT HONG KONG DISNEYLAND**

"For the First Time in Forever," immerse yourself fully in the epic world of the beloved "Frozen" films, brought to life before your very eyes!

**Let It Go Like Never Before at World of Frozen**

Guests are invited to explore the kingdom of Arendelle, from a magical musical boat ride to zooming through Arendelle's picturesque landscape, and Anna and Elsa's hidden forest hideaway. Indulge yourself in delicious cuisine, pick up unique souvenirs, delve deeper into the story of “Frozen” with enchanting attractions and experiences in day and night!

**THE MOST MAGICAL SHOW ON EARTH**

The nighttime spectacular, “Momentous”, illuminates the night sky with about 20 minutes of music and memorable moments. It reminds us how precious time is, and to cherish every moment with our loved ones.

**CASTLE OF MAGICAL DREAMS**

Standing as a shining beacon of courage, hope and possibility, the Castle of Magical Dreams stands proudly as the new centerpiece of Hong Kong Disneyland. It is built on 13 classic Disney tales of Princesses and Queens and inspires everyone to dream big.

**IT'S PLAYTIME WITH DUFFY AND FRIENDS**

Are you ready to jump into a whole new Fans-tasy adventure? Duffy and Friends are dressed in their seasonal outfits, and they want to write new stories with you! They love to spend this colorful time surrounded by laughter, smelling the flowers, touching the grass, bouncing around, jumping for joy, and playing wherever and whenever with you! So, let your innocent self out to play wholeheartedly.

**CAN'T MISS MARVEL EXPERIENCES**

If you are a Marvel fan, be sure to join the heroes in defeating the evil forces of Hydra at the highly-rated Iron Man Experience, and Ant-Man and The Wasp: Nano-Battle!

Fantastic Dining And Shopping Experiences

Feast your eyes and satisfy your appetite with a whimsical assortment of food items inspired by classic Disney Characters or taste the exquisite cuisine integrating authentic flavors from around the world; and bring back a keepsake or two from the merchandise store to commemorate the magical experience.

Stay In The Magic

Extend the Disney spirit for a never-ending magical experience at Hong Kong Disneyland Resort hotels. Whether you are vacationing in a themed suite, spending a romantic staycation, or celebrating a spectacular day. The happiest place on earth becomes the dreami-est place, the delicious-est place, the magic-est place, with iconic Disney Friends at every turn.

Appearance

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Major business houses in Canton

For other uses, see [Hong (disambiguation)](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_(disambiguation) "Hong (disambiguation)").

A **hong** ([Chinese](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 行; [pinyin](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *háng*; [Jyutping](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jyutping "Jyutping"): *hong4-2*) was a type of Chinese [merchant](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Merchant "Merchant") establishment and its associated type of building.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Hongs arose in [Guangzhou](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou") (formerly known as Canton) as intermediaries between Western and Chinese merchants during the 18th and 19th centuries under the [Canton System](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Canton_System "Canton System").

## Canton (Guangzhou)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hong_(business)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Canton (Guangzhou)")]

See also: [Cohong](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cohong "Cohong") and [Thirteen Factories](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thirteen_Factories "Thirteen Factories")

The name "hong" ([Chinese](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 行; [pinyin](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *háng*; [Jyutping](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jyutping "Jyutping"): *hong4*; lit. 'profession', 'row') originally referred to the row of factories built outside of the city walls of [Canton](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou") near the [Pearl River](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pearl_River "Pearl River").[[2]](#cite_note-2) The [Thirteen Factories](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thirteen_Factories "Thirteen Factories") were used during the [Canton System](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Canton_System "Canton System") period to host foreign traders and the products purchased, under the aegis of the *[cohong](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cohong "Cohong")*. The hongs (or Factories) were usually owned by hong merchants such as Pan Zhencheng (also known as [Puankhequa](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Puankhequa "Puankhequa")).[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The Cantonese hongs changed location several times after fires[[4]](#cite_note-4) and became less important after the [First Opium War](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War") (1839–1842), as Canton lost its monopoly of foreign trade and Hong Kong was ceded to the British as a colony.

## Hong Kong

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hong_(business)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Hong Kong")]

In Hong Kong, the name *hong* is used to designate major business houses. One of the earliest foreign hongs established there was [Jardine Matheson & Co.](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_Jardine,_Matheson_&_Co. "History of Jardine, Matheson & Co."), who bought Lot No. 1 at the first Hong Kong land sale in 1841.[[5]](#cite_note-5) In 1843 the same firm established a mainland China headquarters on the [Bund](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Bund_(Shanghai) "The Bund (Shanghai)") in Shanghai, just south of the British Consulate. The building was known as "the Ewo Hong", or "Ewo House", based on the [Cantonese](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese") pronunciation of the company's Chinese name (怡和行, [Cantonese](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese"): *ji4 wo4 hong4*, now [怡和洋行](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jardine_Matheson "Jardine Matheson")).[[6]](#cite_note-6) Jardines took the name from the earlier *[Ewo hong](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ewo_(hong) "Ewo (hong)")* run by [Howqua](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Howqua "Howqua") near Whampoa, Canton.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The term is most often used in reference to [colonial Hong Kong](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong") companies.

Prior to the establishment of banking institutions other than small foreign bank branches, the three firms that financed most of Hong Kong's economic activities were [Jardine Matheson & Co.](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jardine_Matheson_&_Co. "Jardine Matheson & Co."), [Dent & Co.](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dent_&_Co. "Dent & Co."), and [Russell & Company](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Russell_&_Company "Russell & Company").[[8]](#cite_note-Sunzi1H-8) Most of these firms became multinational corporations with management consisting of largely European expatriates.[[9]](#cite_note-Genzberger-9)

By the time of the handover of Hong Kong to China in 1997, many of the hongs had diversified their holdings and shifted their headquarters elsewhere to avoid potential takeover by the [Chinese Communist Party](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Communist_Party_of_China "Communist Party of China").[[9]](#cite_note-Genzberger-9)

## Conglomerates of colonial Hong Kong

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hong_(business)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Conglomerates of colonial Hong Kong")]

Note: *Below are lists of companies that had a predominant effect on Hong Kong's economy at a particular era. Their noteworthiness is debatable. The official names of the era are used.*

### 1843

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hong_(business)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1843")]

* 12 large British firms[[8]](#cite_note-Sunzi1H-8)
* Six Indian [Parsee](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parsee "Parsee") companies – including D.M. Rustomjee
* One American company – [Augustine Heard and Company](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Augustine_Heard_and_Company "Augustine Heard and Company")

### 1844

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hong_(business)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1844")]

* [Jardine Matheson](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_Jardine,_Matheson_&_Co. "History of Jardine, Matheson & Co.")[[8]](#cite_note-Sunzi1H-8)
* [Dent & Co.](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dent_&_Co. "Dent & Co.")

### 1850s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hong_(business)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1850s")]

* [Russell & Co](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Russell_&_Company "Russell & Company") – U.S. company founded by [Samuel Russell](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Samuel_Russell "Samuel Russell") in Canton in 1824 for the opium trade; acquired J & T H Perkins of Boston in 1830, established Hong Kong office in 1850
* [Wheelock Marden](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wheelock_&_Co "Wheelock & Co") 1857

### 1860s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hong_(business)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1860s")]

* Gilman and Bowman – established by Richard James Gilman as a tea trader in 1840; taken over by Duncan Paterson of Australia 1917 and turned into a privately held company; bought by [Inchcape Group](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inchcape_plc "Inchcape plc") in 1969[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### 1870s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hong_(business)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1870s")]

* [Butterfield and Swire](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Swire_Group "Swire Group")[[8]](#cite_note-Sunzi1H-8)
* [Adamson Bell and Company](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dodwell_&_Co. "Dodwell & Co.") - transformed into [Dodwell, Carlill & Co.](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dodwell_&_Co. "Dodwell & Co.") in 1891 by George Benjamin Dodwell; changed name to [Dodwell & Co.](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dodwell_&_Co. "Dodwell & Co.") in 1899; bought by [Inchcape Group](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inchcape_plc "Inchcape plc") in 1972
* Hutchison International - founded in 1877 by John Hutchison, now part of [Hutchison Whampoa](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hutchison_Whampoa "Hutchison Whampoa")

### 1880s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hong_(business)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 1880s")]

* [The Wharf (Holdings)](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Wharf_(Holdings) "The Wharf (Holdings)")

### 1890s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hong_(business)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 1890s")]

* [Jebsen & Co.](//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jebsen_Group "Jebsen Group")

# Han Hong

•

Han Hong is a Chinese singer, songwriter and philanthropist of mixed Tibetan and Han ethnicity. Han is one of the best known Chinese folk singers, incorporating elements of Tibetan music, Jazz, R-n-B, Rock-n-Roll and Latin music in her work. From Wikipedia ([https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Han_Hon...](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Han_Hong_(singer))) under Creative Commons Attribution CC-BY-SA 3.0 ([http://creativecommons.org/licenses/b...](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/legalcode))[More](https://music.youtube.com/)

# hong

## [noun](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/noun)

[ˈhäŋ](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/hong?pronunciation&lang=en_us&dir=h&file=hong0001 "How to pronounce hong (audio)")

ˈhȯŋ

**:**a commercial establishment or house of foreign trade in China

## Word History

Etymology

Chinese (Guangdong) _hòhng_, literally, row

First Known Use

1726, in the meaning defined [above](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/hong#h1)

Time Traveler

[Christopher Chong](https://www.quora.com/profile/Christopher-Chong-1)

Semi-Retired. Ex-Partner of Corporate Finance Firm · Author has 1.7K answers and 17M answer views

·[2y](https://www.quora.com/What-does-Hong-mean-in-Chinese-culture/answer/Christopher-Chong-1)

I do not mean to be difficult, but it depends on which dialect you are using, which tone it refers to and whether there is a second character.

Dialect. The most famous Hong that the world knows is the Hong in Hong Kong (香港). In this context, Hong is a Cantonese word meaning fragrant (in Jyutping it would now be written hoeng1, which is much closer to the way it is pronounced in Cantonese). Thus Hong Kong means fragrant harbour, a name it got because of the trade in agarwood (Aquilaria sinensis). However, I suspect this is not what you are referring to unless you are eluding to 1997 and the even

Continue Reading

I do not mean to be difficult, but it depends on which dialect you are using, which tone it refers to and whether there is a second character.

Dialect. The most famous Hong that the world knows is the Hong in Hong Kong (香港). In this context, Hong is a Cantonese word meaning fragrant (in Jyutping it would now be written hoeng1, which is much closer to the way it is pronounced in Cantonese). Thus Hong Kong means fragrant harbour, a name it got because of the trade in agarwood (Aquilaria sinensis). However, I suspect this is not what you are referring to unless you are eluding to 1997 and the events thereafter.

PS - Hong in English refers to warehouse and was influenced by Cantonese and the fact that there were a lot of waterfront warehouses which the English incorrect though were called Hong.

Tone. If you are referring to Hong in Mandarin, then it matters which tone you are using. Mandarin has 4 tones or inflections and a neutral tone. Meaning the Hong can refer to one of 5 characters. One of these characters is 鸿. Without a second character it is not possible to be certain what it means as it can be an adjective or noun despite the third radical,鸟, meaning bird. That this for example:

两 位 领 导 人 之 间 的 鸿 沟 无 法 弥 合

The sentence reads “the gulf between the two statesmen cannot be bridge”. Here 鸿 is paired with 沟 and means gulf or chasm. On the other hand, 惊 鸿 means graceful.

I suspect the word you are looking for is the hong in 红色 which is pronounced Hóngsè in Mandarin and means red.

Second Character. Even if I got your first character right, the second character can make a lot of difference. While less frequent, this occurs in English as well. For example, the word ‘awe’ is quite different when matched with ‘full’ as in ‘awful’ (though when first coined it did mean full of awe). For example:

红酒 means wine and can refer to any type of western grape wine (as opposed Chinese rice wine)

红薯 means sweet potato, probably because Chinese sweet potatoes had a redness purple skin

I mportance of the colour Red. But as I mentioned above, I suspect the Hong you are looking for is 红色 Hóngsè or the colour red.

You would be right to say that red is everywhere in China today. It is the red envelops given at Chinese New Year, in the red cloth that adorns doors during auspicious occasions, it is widely referred to in poems, songs and literature and is the dominant colour in China’s flag. I could go on for a long time as to why this is but here is a summary:

*   The first reason was that it was easily available. Red hematite rock was readily available and used. The processed powder from such rocks were found in a tomb near Beijing dating back 700,000 years.
*   The earliest use of red in a man made object in China dates back to pottery fragments which came from 5000 to 3000 BC. The pots were coloured black and red, very striking. This suggests that red may have been associated with substance and thus life force in general.
*   In the Han dynasty (202 BC – 9 AD, 25–220 AD), artisans used red dye from madder plants for cloths and for lacquerware.
*   The term red started to appear in literature. The most famous was “Writing a Poem on a Crimson Leaf”. Written during the Tang dynasty (618–907 AD) the story is about a courtesan who writes a love poem on a red leaf which she then casts on the waterway in front of her window. The man finds it and floats a replying poem back. The courtesan falls in love with the unknown writer (its more sentimental that my bland summary). When the courtesan is finally allowed to marry, she discovers that she is married to the man who replied. They lived happy ever after (which was rare for courtesans). Thus red cemented as auspicious and happy.
*   This combination of availability and substance, life, auspiciousness and love reinforced itself. By the time it came to the Qing Dynasty (the last dynasty in China but not a Chinese dynasty) the Qing’s also linked it with fire.
*   Mao liked this association as it meant burning the old and making way for the new or progress. During the height of the Cultural Revolution, the traffic signals were changed so that red was ‘go’ instead of ‘green’.

In summary, the colour red has extremely strong connotations in Chinese culture. The reason appear to be availability and its use in objects that suggested sustenance and live. As more source of red were found, its use was extended and, I suspect, to sell red objects for a higher price, the intangible value was hipped up. Having said that, independent of commerce, Chinese literature started using red as a symbol for good fortune and love. The availability, profitability and literary symbolism reinforced itself. Then after 1949, politics was added until today red means many things and comes with a heavy historical baggage, abet a happy baggage.

… red packets containing money used during Chinese New Year and on auspicious occasions

![Image 2](https://qph.cf2.quoracdn.net/main-qimg-66653b1dbb613d15490b552410cbfe10)

… red cloth over doorway

Hong Kong is a transliterated word from 香港, which literally means fragrant harbor. In the Song and Yuan Dynasty, Hong Kong used to be part of Dongguan, a city of Guangdong province. Since the Ming Dynasty, one harbor of Hong Kong island became an important feeder port of South Guangdong for exporting perfumes produced in Dongguan, thus its name as a fragrant harbor was more and more famous in the South Asia then in the whole world.

Continue Reading

Haha, there’s something you might not believe: despite China being the largest atheist country in the world — and probably the one that values technology and believes in science the most — many Chinese people believe in the power of mantras.

That includes me, someone who cares deeply about mathematics, physics, and engineering.

The most popular mantra is the 准提咒 Zhunti Mantra (Cundi Mantra).

This mantra has no restrictions. You can eat meat, drink alcohol, and even have “illicit sexual relations” (in Buddhist terms, “licit sexual relations” refers only to “normal” intercourse between legally married couple; everything else is “illicit” — different from the legal definition).

You also don’t have to be a Buddhist at all.

As long as you recite this mantra a certain number of times, your wish will come true.

The wish can be purely secular — such as earning more money, recovering from illness, or having a smooth career — with no religious meaning whatsoever.

However, the wish cannot harm others.

For example, wishing someone to fall seriously ill is not allowed.

The wish also shouldn’t be too big, like wishing for world peace — that’s too difficult.

(EDIT:I was wrong. You can pray for world peace, and it will even be praised and encouraged. It’s just that most people who practice this mantra do it to solve their own worldly problems, such as wanting to make more money.）

This mantra is very short; you can recite it in just a moment.

![Image 21](https://qph.cf2.quoracdn.net/main-qimg-035501e4492bb88ac6817782a65eaae8)

The picture shows the phonetic transcription.

As for how many repetitions are needed for results, it’s hard to say.

Some say they wished for their body odor to disappear and it took only 5,000 recitations.

Others say they wanted their child to turn over a new leaf, so the couple together recited it 800,000 times.

If you want to give it a try but don’t know how to pronounce it, I found a pronunciation version on YouTube — you can follow along a few times, and it’s so short you’ll have it memorized in no time.

If you don’t like this tone or rhythm, that’s fine — you can search for “准提咒” in Chinese, and you’ll find plenty of different recitations. Just pick one you like.

There are many counting methods. Traditionally, people used prayer beads. Nowadays, most people just buy a finger counter from Taobao: for example, press once every 4 recitations; when the counter reaches 100, that means you’ve recited it 400 times.

![Image 22](https://qph.cf2.quoracdn.net/main-qimg-16d618eeaa674f1bfc4e2c8f3b5d7e43)

Why do I recite it? Because it works.

As Comrade Deng Xiaoping famously said: “It doesn’t matter whether a cat is black or white; as long as it catches mice, it’s a good cat.”

The premise of his statement was that at the time, China was in the midst of a heated debate over whether certain practices counted as “capitalist.”

For example, according to Marx’s writings, hiring more than seven workers would already classify someone as a capitalist — yet some private enterprises at the time were employing over 200 people.

It was in this context that Deng Xiaoping made his famous assertion: “Let’s not argue for now about whether it is capitalism; let’s see whether it can raise production levels.”

It’s a bit like this mantra: don’t argue over whether it’s superstition — just see if it works.

At the time, many “capitalists” were deeply worried about the possibility of being politically targeted or having their assets confiscated in the future. Some officials came up with a way to ease their fears: they asked the capitalists to write a letter stating they were willing to donate their factories to the state free of charge. The officials would then write an official note on it: “Very good, very patriotic — we will not accept it for now.”

With this signed note in hand, the “capitalists” felt much more at ease. Even if there was political persecution in the future, they could show the document and say: “Look, I’ve been patriotic all along — I was ready to donate my factory to our great motherland long ago!”

Looking back at the 1980s now, it truly feels like a different world.

~~

It’s the same with the Zhunti Mantra — if it solves my real-life problems, that’s what matters most.

As for the underlying principle… I’m curious about that too.

~~~

My personal guess goes like this: Suppose there is a deserted island (Earth), and countless people have unfortunately been stranded there, all having suffered head injuries that caused amnesia.

At this point, a rescuer (the Buddha) voluntarily comes to the island and tells you, “Keep writing SOS in the sand.” You’re skeptical, but one day, an iron bird (a helicopter) actually comes to rescue you!

Advanced liberation-type mantras are like SOS—they allow you to directly leave this miserable world.

But many people don’t believe it, so in order to make you believe, the rescuer hides up in a tree and secretly picks a coconut, then tells you, “If you sincerely recite a certain mantra (the Cundi Mantra), you’ll have food to eat.”

You try reciting it in reverse disbelief, and the rescuer drops a coconut.

To your surprise, it really works!

At that point, you might start having faith in SOS as well.

That’s my explanation.

Continue Reading

My wife, who is Chinese, and I have discussed this topic, but not to a great extent.

In my view (as an outsider looking in), Chinese culture has endured several upheavals:

1.   Chinese culture is very rich and centuries (if not millennia) old
2.   Having a very rich and very old culture builds invisible restrictions in activity, flexibility, and outward initiative - the very things you celebrate are the very things that keep you from progressing.
3.   The Cultural Revolution, for all its excesses and faults (and there were many), sought to shake up the established order and allow the creation of a new paradigm for China, but it could never be permanently enforced nor should it have been - it was brutal but effective as a temporary movement, it would have been disastrous as a permanent one. More like a “cultural tsunami” if you will.
4.   The Post-Cultural Revolution allowed the parts of the new paradigm to form a newer foundation (in the economy, education, science, technology and other vital sectors)
5.   The Post-Post Cultural Restoration was to allow the resplendent and vibrant (and ancient) culture to be celebrated on top of the newer established paradigm

China is a blend of centuries old tradition and twentieth century modernity: historic temples and pagodas surrounded by modern skyscrapers, a myriad street food and sprawling ancient imperial palaces, as well as striking geological formations.

Chinese people tend to honour tradition, but they also embrace aspects of Western popular culture.

Thanks in part to the enduring influence of Confucianism, Chinese customs include a strong sense of family, a deep respect for hierarchy, and an appreciation for harmony over conflict. (The millennials and Gen Z are attempting to breakaway from the traditional, as the nation experiences challenges like never before.)

China is a fascinating intersection of ancient cultures and language and the arts, with ultramodern infrastructure, innovation, and growth.

What Chinese people do in China is more or less similar to what people in the rest of the world do, albeit with a Chinese characteristics.

'Being Chinese" is complex and aspirational identity influenced by various factors, including culture, history, and personal experience:

Chinese culture is influenced by Confucianism and emphasizes family, hierarchy, harmony, filial piety, and loyalty. It also places a strong emphasis on respecting elders.

Some people may be content in their Chinese identity, while others may feel inadequate. They venture off shores.

Assumptions and expectations of a Chinese person can evolve as China's fortunes change over time.

Some people say that Chineseness can have a strong moral appeal, and that it can be more likely to elicit recognition of its moral character when it's actualized through a perpetual struggle to become a better moral being.

Despite everything that's said it really doesn't answer your question does it?

I'll try to clear it up a bit… as a practising Chinese!

Chinese people are sticklers of centuries old traditions and festivals and festivities..Festivals, such as the Chinese New Year aka Lunar New Year, and Spring Festival.

They are a reflection of the country's traditions and values.

Chinese appreciate art and it includes painting, sculpture, architecture, pottery, jade carving, and numerous other fine art forms.

Chinese culture places a strong emphasis on respecting and honouring elders. It is common practice to address older individuals with proper titles.

Chinese elevate calligraphy to an art form. The ink brush, ink, paper, and inkstone are the essential elements of Chinese calligraphy; and it has so many different characteristics and grade.

![Image 26](https://qph.cf2.quoracdn.net/main-qimg-2b46ffe56107f2b6553252e4db4b7ced)

Chinese love to drink tea, which is more than just a drink. It's a conduit for cultural exchange. A Gong Fu Cha (工夫茶) ceremony is a way to observe the artistry and respect for the tradition of tea drinking in China.

Chinese love to eat!

In Chinese culture, food is placed in the middle of the table and everyone shares. A typical meal starts with appetizers, cold dishes, followed by several dishes - various meat, plant-based food and fresh cooked vegetables. That's usually for dinner.

Chinese people adhere to traditional festivals for many reasons, including their religious and spiritual beliefs, and the meaning behind the festivals.

Some traditional Chinese festivals include:

![Image 27](https://qph.cf2.quoracdn.net/main-qimg-7737ac01603424bce97387cbfc31845e)

*   Dragon Boat Festival (Duan Wu Jie is also known as Duan Yang, which means “Upright Sun” or “Double Fifth”)

It is celebrated on the 5th day of the 5th lunar month. This festival commemorates the death of an ancient famous Chinese scholar, 屈原 Qu Yuan. Duan Wu Jie is celebrated more in the south than the northern region.
*   Mid-Autumn Festival

Celebrated on the 15th day of the 8th lunar month, this festival features moon cakes, which symbolize family reunion.
*   Chinese New Year

Also known as the Spring Festival, this is the most important festival in China. It's celebrated for 15 days and is a time for family reunions, feasting, and gift-giving. This time round it falls on January 25–26th 2025.
*   Qingming Festival

This important annual festival involves visiting ancestral tombs to sweep them and make offerings.
*   Qixi Festival

Also known as the Qiqiao Festival or Seven Sister's Birthday, this festival is celebrated on the 7th day of the 7th lunar month. It's based on the legend of the Cowherd and the Weaver Girl.

Other traditional Chinese festival customs include:

![Image 28](https://qph.cf2.quoracdn.net/main-qimg-854eb9c4973454046b0b24d68d0d887d)

*   Pasting scrolls, the character "Fu", meaning “luck”, and paper-cut red coloured pictures
*   Setting off firecrackers and fireworks, and lion dance performance
*   Paying New Year visits to family relatives and friends

![Image 29](https://qph.cf2.quoracdn.net/main-qimg-6acb1fd2b16a56b28288a141c56afeb1)

*   Eating jiaozi (dumplings). If I were to pick a food that says “Chinese” it'd be this!
*   Watching lanterns and fireworks
*   Performing folk dances
*   Eating yuanxiao or tang yuan glutinous rice balls with a sweet filling. This is the iconic food during the Lantern Festival (15th day of the Spring Festival); as well as the Winter Solstice Festival (dongzhi) this year it was on 21 December.

![Image 30](https://qph.cf2.quoracdn.net/main-qimg-d0bbdfbe54d88cc1dfc6e88bc9f120e0)

*   I just had my tang yuan a few days ago, in Kuala Lumpur.

·[3y](https://www.quora.com/What-does-HK-mean-in-Chinese/answer/RenShen-1?no_redirect=1)

This is a vague question. My impression is that you would have intended to mean Hong Kong.

If you do, then Hong Kong in Chinese would be 香港 or xianggang. In English, it means Fragrant Harbour due to the perfume trade back in pre-colonial days.

Upvote · 

9 2

I do not know, but “Hong Kong" means “fragrant harbour".

# Definition of 'hong'

COBUILD frequency band

## hong in American English

 (hɔŋ[](https://www.collinsdictionary.com/us/dictionary/english/hong "Pronunciation for hong"), hɑŋ[](https://www.collinsdictionary.com/us/dictionary/english/hong "Pronunciation for hong"))

[](https://www.facebook.com/sharer.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.collinsdictionary.com%2Fus%2Fdictionary%2Fenglish%2Fhong "Share this page on Facebook")[](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.collinsdictionary.com%2Fus%2Fdictionary%2Fenglish%2Fhong&via=collinsdict "Share this page on Twitter")

noun Origin: Chin hōng, a row, series, factory

 history

 in China, a warehouse or factory

## Hong in American English

 (hɑŋ[](https://www.collinsdictionary.com/us/dictionary/english/hong "Pronunciation for Hong"), hɔŋ[](https://www.collinsdictionary.com/us/dictionary/english/hong "Pronunciation for Hong"))

## hong in American English

 (hɑŋ)

[](https://www.facebook.com/sharer.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.collinsdictionary.com%2Fus%2Fdictionary%2Fenglish%2Fhong "Share this page on Facebook")[](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.collinsdictionary.com%2Fus%2Fdictionary%2Fenglish%2Fhong&via=collinsdict "Share this page on Twitter")

noun

1.(in China)

a group of [rooms](https://www.collinsdictionary.com/us/dictionary/english/room "Definition of rooms") or [buildings](https://www.collinsdictionary.com/us/dictionary/english/building "Definition of buildings") forming a warehouse, factory, etc

one of the factories under foreign [ownership](https://www.collinsdictionary.com/us/dictionary/english/ownership "Definition of ownership") formerly [maintained](https://www.collinsdictionary.com/us/dictionary/english/maintain "Definition of maintained") at [Canton](https://www.collinsdictionary.com/us/dictionary/english/canton "Definition of Canton")

## English

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: English")]

English [Wikipedia](/wiki/Wikipedia "Wikipedia") has an article on:

**[hong (business)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/hong_(business) "w:hong (business)")**

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/hong_(business) "w:hong (business)")

### Alternative forms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Alternative forms")]

[yhonge](/wiki/yhonge "yhonge"), [ihonge](/w/index.php?title=ihonge&action=edit&redlink=1 "ihonge (page does not exist)"), [henge](/wiki/henge "henge"), [yhenge](/w/index.php?title=yhenge&action=edit&redlink=1 "yhenge (page does not exist)"), [hyng](/w/index.php?title=hyng&action=edit&redlink=1 "hyng (page does not exist)"), [hing](/wiki/hing "hing"), [honge](/w/index.php?title=honge&action=edit&redlink=1 "honge (page does not exist)"), [ȝonge](/wiki/%C8%9Donge "ȝonge"), [ȝenge](/wiki/%C8%9Denge "ȝenge"), [háng](/wiki/h%C3%A1ng "háng"),

### Etymology 1

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Etymology 1")]

(This [etymology](/wiki/Wiktionary:Etymology "Wiktionary:Etymology") is missing or incomplete. Please add to it, or discuss it at the [Etymology scriptorium](/wiki/Wiktionary:Etymology_scriptorium#hong "Wiktionary:Etymology scriptorium").)

#### Verb

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Verb")]

**hong**

1. ([obsolete](/wiki/Appendix:Glossary#obsolete "Appendix:Glossary")) [past](/wiki/Appendix:Glossary#past_tense "Appendix:Glossary") of *[hang](/wiki/hang#English "hang")*
   * **1590**, [Edmund Spenser](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Edmund_Spenser "w:Edmund Spenser"), “Book II, Canto VII”, in [The Faerie Queene. […]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Faerie_Queene "w:The Faerie Queene"), London:  […]  [[John Wolfe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Wolfe_(printer) "w:John Wolfe (printer)")] for [William Ponsonbie](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Ponsonby_(publisher) "w:William Ponsonby (publisher)"), [→OCLC](https://search.worldcat.org/title/960102938):

     :   Lyke an huge cave hewne out of rocky clifte,   
         From whose rough vaut the ragged breaches **hong**

### Etymology 2

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Etymology 2")]

From [Cantonese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cantonese "w:Cantonese") *[行](/wiki/%E8%A1%8C#Cantonese "行")* (hong, “trade, business”). (This [etymology](/wiki/Wiktionary:Etymology "Wiktionary:Etymology") is missing or incomplete. Please add to it, or discuss it at the [Etymology scriptorium](/wiki/Wiktionary:Etymology_scriptorium#hong "Wiktionary:Etymology scriptorium"). Particularly: “Could someone with knowledge of Cantonese verify this etymology? Is it a shortening of 洋行”)

#### Pronunciation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Pronunciation")]

* ([UK](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/British_English "w:British English")) [IPA](/wiki/Wiktionary:International_Phonetic_Alphabet "Wiktionary:International Phonetic Alphabet")([key](/wiki/Appendix:English_pronunciation "Appendix:English pronunciation")): /hɒŋ/
* ([General American](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/General_American_English "w:General American English")) [IPA](/wiki/Wiktionary:International_Phonetic_Alphabet "Wiktionary:International Phonetic Alphabet")([key](/wiki/Appendix:English_pronunciation "Appendix:English pronunciation")): /hɔŋ/
  + ([*cot*–*caught* merger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cot%E2%80%93caught_merger "w:Cot–caught merger")) [IPA](/wiki/Wiktionary:International_Phonetic_Alphabet "Wiktionary:International Phonetic Alphabet")([key](/wiki/Appendix:English_pronunciation "Appendix:English pronunciation")): /hɑŋ/
* Rhymes: [-ɒŋ](/wiki/Rhymes:English/%C9%92%C5%8B "Rhymes:English/ɒŋ")

#### Noun

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Noun")]

**hong** (*plural* **[hongs](/wiki/hongs#English "hongs")**)

1. A foreign trading company in [China](/wiki/China "China").
   * **1901**, Hezekiah Butterworth, “1.Strange Things to Be Seen in China – Jataka Tales – the Boxers – American Tea-farms”, in [Traveller Tales of China or the Story-Telling Hongs](/wiki/File:Traveller_tales_of_China,_or,_The_story-telling_hongs_(IA_cu31924023140944).pdf "File:Traveller tales of China, or, The story-telling hongs (IA cu31924023140944).pdf"), Boston: Dana Estes & Company, page 11:

     :   "You and your son are about to visit China," said a Chinese agent of an old and established **hong** in Canton.  […]  The speaker was Ah Hue, or Ah Hue-Ling. He had made a reputation for honorable dealing as an agent of the tea trade.
   * **1935**, William Ukers, All About Tea:

     :   In this partly manufactured state they are sold to collectors, who re-sell them to tea **hongs**, or factories, and these **hongs** in turn resell to middlemen, who supply the foreign exporter.
   * **1997** August 7, “Superman versus the hong”, in The Economist‎[[1]](https://www.economist.com/business/1997/08/07/superman-versus-the-hong), [→ISSN](https://www.worldcat.org/issn/0013-0613):

     :   Even though China's red flag now flutters above them, some 40% of the offices are still owned by Hongkong Land, an immensely wealthy company that is part of the Jardine Matheson group, the biggest of the British-owned “**hongs**” and the one that did most to establish the British territory.

### See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: See also")]

etymologicaly unrelated terms

* [hongbao](/wiki/hongbao#English "hongbao")
* [hong boat](/wiki/hong_boat#English "hong boat")

### Anagrams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Anagrams")]

* [Gohn](/wiki/Gohn#English "Gohn")

## Hokkien

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Hokkien")]

### Etymology 1

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Etymology 1")]

|  |
| --- |
| **For pronunciation and definitions of** **hong** **– see [坊](/wiki/%E5%9D%8A#Chinese "坊") (“[lane](/wiki/lane "lane"); [alley](/wiki/alley "alley"); [store](/wiki/store "store"); etc.”).** (*This term is the [pe̍h-ōe-jī](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pe%CC%8Dh-%C5%8De-j%C4%AB "w:Pe̍h-ōe-jī") form of* [坊](/wiki/%E5%9D%8A#Chinese "坊")). |

### Etymology 2

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Etymology 2")]

|  |
| --- |
| **For pronunciation and definitions of** **hong** **– see [封](/wiki/%E5%B0%81#Chinese "封") (“[border](/wiki/border "border"), [boundary](/wiki/boundary "boundary"); [state](/wiki/state "state"), [domain](/wiki/domain "domain"), [territory](/wiki/territory "territory"); etc.”).** (*This term is the [pe̍h-ōe-jī](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pe%CC%8Dh-%C5%8De-j%C4%AB "w:Pe̍h-ōe-jī") form of* [封](/wiki/%E5%B0%81#Chinese "封")). |

### Etymology 3

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Etymology 3")]

|  |
| --- |
| **For pronunciation and definitions of** **hong** **– see [峰](/wiki/%E5%B3%B0#Chinese "峰") (“[peak](/wiki/peak "peak"); [summit](/wiki/summit "summit"); [hump](/wiki/hump "hump"); etc.”).** (*This term is the [pe̍h-ōe-jī](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pe%CC%8Dh-%C5%8De-j%C4%AB "w:Pe̍h-ōe-jī") form of* [峰](/wiki/%E5%B3%B0#Chinese "峰")). |

### Etymology 4

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Etymology 4")]

## Indonesian

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Indonesian")]

Indonesian [Wikipedia](/wiki/Wikipedia "Wikipedia") has an article on:

**[hong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/id:hong "w:id:hong")**

[Wikipedia id](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/id:hong "w:id:hong")

### Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Etymology")]

[Borrowed](/wiki/Appendix:Glossary#loanword "Appendix:Glossary") from [Hokkien](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hokkien "w:Hokkien") [鳳凰](/wiki/%E9%B3%B3%E5%87%B0#Chinese "鳳凰")／[凤凰](/wiki/%E5%87%A4%E5%87%B0#Chinese "凤凰") (*hōng-hông*, “[fenghuang](/wiki/fenghuang "fenghuang")”).

### Pronunciation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Pronunciation")]

* ([Standard Indonesian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indonesian_language#Phonology "w:Indonesian language")) [IPA](/wiki/Wiktionary:International_Phonetic_Alphabet "Wiktionary:International Phonetic Alphabet")([key](/wiki/Appendix:Indonesian_pronunciation "Appendix:Indonesian pronunciation")): /ˈhoŋ/ [ˈhoŋ]
* Rhymes: [-oŋ](/w/index.php?title=Rhymes:Indonesian/o%C5%8B&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rhymes:Indonesian/oŋ (page does not exist)")
* Syllabification: hong

### Noun

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Noun")]

**hong** (*plural* ****hong**-**hong****)

1. ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese "Chinese") [mythology](/wiki/mythology "mythology")) [fenghuang](/wiki/fenghuang "fenghuang"): a mythological Chinese chimerical bird whose physical body symbolizes the six celestial bodies

   :   Synonym: [fenghuang](/wiki/fenghuang#Indonesian "fenghuang")
2. ([mythology](/wiki/mythology "mythology")) [phoenix](/wiki/phoenix "phoenix"): a mythological bird, said to be the only one of its kind, which lives for 500 years and then dies by burning to ashes on a pyre of its own making, ignited by the sun. It then arises anew from the ashes

   :   Synonyms: [feniks](/wiki/feniks#Indonesian "feniks"), [foniks](/wiki/foniks#Indonesian "foniks"), [funiks](/wiki/funiks#Indonesian "funiks")

## Vietnamese

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=64 "Edit section: Vietnamese")]

### Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=65 "Edit section: Etymology")]

Compare [Chinese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chinese_language "w:Chinese language") *[烘](/wiki/%E7%83%98#Chinese "烘")* (hōng), [Thai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thai_language "w:Thai language") *[แห้ง](/wiki/%E0%B9%81%E0%B8%AB%E0%B9%89%E0%B8%87#Thai "แห้ง")* (hɛ̂ɛng).

### Pronunciation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=66 "Edit section: Pronunciation")]

* ([Hà Nội](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hanoi "w:Hanoi")) [IPA](/wiki/Wiktionary:International_Phonetic_Alphabet "Wiktionary:International Phonetic Alphabet")([key](/wiki/Appendix:Vietnamese_pronunciation "Appendix:Vietnamese pronunciation")): [hawŋ͡m˧˧]
* ([Huế](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hu%E1%BA%BF "w:Huế")) [IPA](/wiki/Wiktionary:International_Phonetic_Alphabet "Wiktionary:International Phonetic Alphabet")([key](/wiki/Appendix:Vietnamese_pronunciation "Appendix:Vietnamese pronunciation")): [hawŋ͡m˧˧] ~ [hɔŋ˧˧]
* ([Saigon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saigon "w:Saigon")) [IPA](/wiki/Wiktionary:International_Phonetic_Alphabet "Wiktionary:International Phonetic Alphabet")([key](/wiki/Appendix:Vietnamese_pronunciation "Appendix:Vietnamese pronunciation")): [hawŋ͡m˧˧]

### Verb

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=67 "Edit section: Verb")]

**hong** [•](/wiki/Wiktionary:Vietnamese_transliteration "Wiktionary:Vietnamese transliteration") ([𣈞](/wiki/%F0%A3%88%9E#Vietnamese "𣈞"), [𤈤](/w/index.php?title=%F0%A4%88%A4&action=edit&redlink=1 "𤈤 (page does not exist)"))

1. to [dry](/wiki/dry "dry") (in the [wind](/wiki/wind "wind"), [sun](/wiki/sun "sun"), or near the [fire](/wiki/fire "fire"))

## Zhuang

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=72 "Edit section: Zhuang")]

### Pronunciation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=73 "Edit section: Pronunciation")]

* ([Standard Zhuang](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standard_Zhuang "w:Standard Zhuang")) [IPA](/wiki/Wiktionary:International_Phonetic_Alphabet "Wiktionary:International Phonetic Alphabet")([key](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zhuang_phonology "wikipedia:Zhuang phonology")): /hoːŋ˨˦/
* Tone numbers: hong1
* Hyphenation: hong

### Etymology 1

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=74 "Edit section: Etymology 1")]

From [Chinese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chinese_language "w:Chinese language") [工](/wiki/%E5%B7%A5#Chinese "工") (*[OC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Old_Chinese "w:Old Chinese") \*koːŋ*).

#### Noun

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=75 "Edit section: Noun")]

**hong** (*Sawndip forms* **[𰏊](/w/index.php?title=%F0%B0%8F%8A&action=edit&redlink=1 "𰏊 (page does not exist)")** *or* **[⿱功大](/w/index.php?title=%E2%BF%B1%E5%8A%9F%E5%A4%A7&action=edit&redlink=1 "⿱功大 (page does not exist)")** *or* **[慌](/wiki/%E6%85%8C#Zhuang "慌")** *or* **[⿰工空](/w/index.php?title=%E2%BF%B0%E5%B7%A5%E7%A9%BA&action=edit&redlink=1 "⿰工空 (page does not exist)")** *or* **[⿱红工](/w/index.php?title=%E2%BF%B1%E7%BA%A2%E5%B7%A5&action=edit&redlink=1 "⿱红工 (page does not exist)")** *or* **[啌](/wiki/%E5%95%8C#Zhuang "啌")** *or* **[空](/wiki/%E7%A9%BA#Zhuang "空")** *or* **[康](/wiki/%E5%BA%B7#Zhuang "康")** *or* **[𭘎](/w/index.php?title=%F0%AD%98%8E&action=edit&redlink=1 "𭘎 (page does not exist)")** *or* **[⿱䒑巷](/w/index.php?title=%E2%BF%B1%E4%92%91%E5%B7%B7&action=edit&redlink=1 "⿱䒑巷 (page does not exist)")** *or* **[控](/wiki/%E6%8E%A7#Zhuang "控")**, *1957–1982 spelling* **[hoŋ](/w/index.php?title=ho%C5%8B&action=edit&redlink=1 "hoŋ (page does not exist)")**)

1. [work](/wiki/work "work"); [labor](/wiki/labor "labor")

   :   Synonym: [goeng](/wiki/goeng#Zhuang "goeng")

##### Derived terms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=76 "Edit section: Derived terms")]

### Etymology 2

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=77 "Edit section: Etymology 2")]

From [Proto-Tai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proto-Tai_language "w:Proto-Tai language") *[\*χɔːŋᴬ](/wiki/Reconstruction:Proto-Tai/%CF%87%C9%94%CB%90%C5%8B%E1%B4%AC "Reconstruction:Proto-Tai/χɔːŋᴬ")* (“thing”). Cognate with [Thai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thai_language "w:Thai language") *[ของ](/wiki/%E0%B8%82%E0%B8%AD%E0%B8%87#Thai "ของ")* (kɔ̌ɔng), [Northern Thai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lanna_language "w:Lanna language") *[ᨡᩬᨦ](/wiki/%E1%A8%A1%E1%A9%AC%E1%A8%A6#Northern_Thai "ᨡᩬᨦ")*, [Lao](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lao_language "w:Lao language") *[ຂອງ](/wiki/%E0%BA%82%E0%BA%AD%E0%BA%87#Lao "ຂອງ")* (khǭng), [Lü](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tai_Lue_language "w:Tai Lue language") *[ᦃᦸᧂ](/w/index.php?title=%E1%A6%83%E1%A6%B8%E1%A7%82&action=edit&redlink=1 "ᦃᦸᧂ (page does not exist)")* (ẋoang), [Tai Dam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tai_Dam_language "w:Tai Dam language") *[ꪄꪮꪉ](/w/index.php?title=%EA%AA%84%EA%AA%AE%EA%AA%89&action=edit&redlink=1 "ꪄꪮꪉ (page does not exist)")*, [Shan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shan_language "w:Shan language") *[ၶွင်](/wiki/%E1%81%B6%E1%80%BD%E1%80%84%E1%80%BA#Shan "ၶွင်")* (khǎung), [Zuojiang Zhuang](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zuojiang_Zhuang_languages "w:Zuojiang Zhuang languages") *[kong](/wiki/kong#Zuojiang_Zhuang "kong")*.

#### Noun

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=78 "Edit section: Noun")]

**hong** (*1957–1982 spelling* **[hoŋ](/w/index.php?title=ho%C5%8B&action=edit&redlink=1 "hoŋ (page does not exist)")**)

1. ([dialectal](/wiki/Appendix:Glossary#dialectal "Appendix:Glossary")) [thing](/wiki/thing "thing"); [object](/wiki/object "object")

   :   Synonyms: [doenghyiengh](/wiki/doenghyiengh#Zhuang "doenghyiengh"), [doxgaiq](/wiki/doxgaiq#Zhuang "doxgaiq"), (dialectal) [swhyiengh](/w/index.php?title=swhyiengh&action=edit&redlink=1 "swhyiengh (page does not exist)")

## Zou

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=79 "Edit section: Zou")]

### Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=80 "Edit section: Etymology")]

From [Proto-Kuki-Chin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proto-Kuki-Chin_language "w:Proto-Kuki-Chin language") *[\*hoŋ](/w/index.php?title=Reconstruction:Proto-Kuki-Chin/ho%C5%8B&action=edit&redlink=1 "Reconstruction:Proto-Kuki-Chin/hoŋ (page does not exist)")*. Cognate with [Mizo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mizo_language "w:Mizo language") *[hàwng](/w/index.php?title=h%C3%A0wng&action=edit&redlink=1 "hàwng (page does not exist)")*.

### Pronunciation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=81 "Edit section: Pronunciation")]

* [IPA](/wiki/Wiktionary:International_Phonetic_Alphabet "Wiktionary:International Phonetic Alphabet")([key](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zou_phonology "wikipedia:Zou phonology")): [hōŋ]
* Hyphenation: hong

### Verb

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=hong&action=edit&section=82 "Edit section: Verb")]

**hong** (*stem II* **[hòn](/wiki/hon#Zou "hon")**)

1. ([transitive](/wiki/Appendix:Glossary#transitive "Appendix:Glossary")) to [open](/wiki/open "open")
